title: Chocolate éclairs
subtitle: Choux pastry fingers filled with vanilla cream and topped with chocolate
excerpt: Chocolate éclairs: piped choux pastry baked until hollow and crisp, filled with lightly sweetened whipped cream, and dipped in a dark chocolate ganache. Three components, all straightforward. The choux is the key: it must be dried out thoroughly so the shells hold their shape.
sourceNotes: Éclairs are a French pâtisserie tradition documented from the nineteenth century, when the name first appears in French domestic cookery guides. Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903), documents the éclair in its modern form: choux pastry finger, cream filling, chocolate fondant top. The domestic simplified version (cream rather than crème pâtissière, ganache rather than fondant) is widely reproduced in twentieth-century British and French domestic baking guides and is fully public-domain.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the unfilled shells. Defrost at room temperature, re-crisp in a 160°C oven for 5 minutes, then fill.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake the shells the day before and keep in an airtight container. Fill on the day.
recipe.temperatureNote: fan oven; reduce to 180°C after the first 15 minutes

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, 15 minutes at 200°C then 20 to 25 minutes at 180°C until deep gold and hollow-sounding

glossaryTerms.definition: Choux pastry is hollow because steam created inside the dough puffs it open during baking. For this to work, the shell must set dry and crisp before it collapses back. Two techniques help: first, reduce the oven temperature after the initial puff so the shell dries without burning; second, after baking, pierce the base of each shell with a skewer and return to the oven for 5 minutes. The hole lets steam escape and the shell crisps from inside. Shells that deflate on cooling were underbaked or not dried. A correctly baked shell sounds hollow when tapped.

content.text: Choux pastry looks technical but has only four ingredients and one technique: drying out the paste before adding eggs. Do not open the oven door during baking. The shells need full, uninterrupted heat to puff and set.
Make and bake the choux, 35–40 minutes

Preheat the oven to 200°C fan. Bring 200 ml water and the {{unsalted-butter}} to a rolling boil in a saucepan. Remove from the heat and immediately beat in the {{plain-flour}} until the mixture leaves the sides of the pan cleanly. Return to medium heat and stir vigorously for 2 minutes to dry the paste. Transfer to a bowl. Add the beaten {{eggs}} a little at a time, beating well between additions — the paste should be smooth and fall from a spoon in a thick V. Pipe 12 lines about 10 cm long onto a lined baking tray. Bake for 15 minutes, then reduce to 180°C fan and bake for a further 20 to 25 minutes. See the
note: pierce the base of each shell and return to the oven for 5 minutes. Cool on a rack.

Whip the {{double-cream}} (filling portion) with the {{icing-sugar}} to soft peaks. Slit each shell along the side and pipe or spoon in the cream. For the ganache, heat the {{double-cream}} (glaze portion) to a simmer and pour over the chopped {{dark-chocolate}}. Leave for 2 minutes, then stir until smooth. Dip the top of each filled éclair into the ganache. Set on a rack for 30 minutes before serving.
